supporting_evidence: CRITICAL: 81F on warfarin with direct head trauma — this is the classic setup for acute or subacute subdural hematoma
Age-related cerebral atrophy creates space for hematoma expansion before symptoms appear
Bridging veins are stretched and fragile in elderly patients with brain atrophy
Warfarin prevents clot formation, allowing continued bleeding from even minor venous tears
The lucid interval is the most dangerous feature — patient appears well for hours before rapid deterioration
Mortality of anticoagulant-associated subdural hematoma approaches 60% if not detected early
On warfarin, assume bleeding until proven otherwise
Epidural hematoma from temporal bone fracture — osteoporotic bone fractures more easily
Traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage possible from impact mechanism
Hypertension (155/88) compounds hemorrhagic risk — hypertensive patients bleed more and stop bleeding less effectively
Dizziness is a red-flag symptom that could represent early mass effect or posterior fossa pathology
81F with osteoporosis + fall + hip pain = hip fracture until proven otherwise
Neck of femur fractures are the most common osteoporotic fracture requiring hospitalisation
Warfarin complicates surgical timing — INR must be corrected before operative fixation
Delayed diagnosis of occult hip fracture (X-ray negative) leads to increased morbidity — MRI indicated if clinical suspicion persists
30-day mortality for hip fracture in 80+ age group is 8-10%
Head strike on floor implies cervical spine was loaded during the fall
Osteoporosis affects cervical vertebrae — odontoid (dens) fractures are the most common cervical fracture in elderly
Odontoid fractures can be stable initially but catastrophically unstable with movement
Canadian C-spine Rule: age >65 = mandatory imaging regardless of symptoms
Spinal epidural hematoma in anticoagulated patient with cervical trauma is a neurosurgical emergency
The fall itself may be secondary to a cardiac event — known atrial fibrillation
Paroxysmal AF or rapid ventricular response can cause transient cerebral hypoperfusion
Enalapril can potentiate orthostatic hypotension in elderly with impaired baroreflexes
If syncope caused the fall, recurrence risk is immediate and high
ECG, continuous telemetry, and orthostatic vitals are mandatory to evaluate this pathway

reasoning: DEVIL'S ADVOCATE ASSESSMENT: On warfarin, assume bleeding until proven otherwise. This 81-year-old anticoagulated female with head trauma, hip pain, and dizziness is a convergence of high-risk factors that demands aggressive investigation. Every candidate in this differential is flagged as don't-miss because the consequences of missing ANY of these diagnoses are severe or fatal. Subdural hematoma is my highest-probability diagnosis — not because it is statistically the most common outcome of falls in the elderly, but because it is the diagnosis most likely to kill this specific patient if missed. The warfarin + head trauma + age triad is the highest-risk combination in emergency trauma medicine for occult intracranial bleeding. Hip fracture ranks third but carries its own mortality — delayed surgical fixation due to undetected fracture or warfarin reversal delays increases 30-day mortality. C-spine fracture with potential spinal epidural hematoma in an anticoagulated patient could result in permanent quadriplegia. Even the cardiac syncope pathway is flagged because if the fall was caused by an arrhythmia, the patient is at risk of another syncopal event and further injury. This patient needs: immediate CT head, C-spine imaging, hip X-ray (with MRI if negative but suspicious), urgent INR, ECG, and continuous neurological monitoring. Do not be reassured by the stable vitals and GCS 15 — the lucid interval is real and lethal.
